data.title: Here's How Much $100 Invested In Freeport-McMoRan 10 Years Ago Would Be Worth Today
data.description:
Freeport-McMoRan (NYSE:FCX) has outperformed the market over the past 10 years by 6.0% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 18.8%. Currently, Freeport-McMoRan has a market capitalization of $98.94 billion.

Buying $100 In FCX: If an investor had bought $100 of FCX stock 10 years ago, it would be worth $576.19 today based on a price of $68.84 for FCX at the time of writing.

Freeport-McMoRan's Performance Over Last 10 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.

This article was generated by Benzinga's automated content engine and reviewed by an editor.

data.title: Aehr Test Systems, CareDx, Okta And Other Big Stocks Moving Higher On Thursday
data.description:
U.S. stocks were mixed, with the Dow Jones index gaining around 0.1% on Thursday.

Shares of Aehr Test Systems (NASDAQ:AEHR) rose sharply following a record $41 million production order from a lead hyperscale AI customer.

The significant order is expected to enhance the company's position in the semiconductor testing market as it ramps up production capabilities for AI processors, which is reflected in the stock's strong performance today as broader markets edged higher.

Aehr Test Systems shares jumped 15.6% to $84.65 on Thursday.

Here are some other big stocks recording gains in today’s session.

Photo via Shutterstock

data.title: Why Is Allbirds Stock Dropping Today? Traders Fade NewBird AI Pivot After 600% Surge
data.description:
Allbirds Inc (NASDAQ:BIRD) shares are trading lower Thursday morning after Wednesday's 600% regular-session rip and a 25% after-hours drop signaled traders were fading the "sneakers-to-servers" reset.

The pullback follows a $50 million convertible facility and a planned rebrand to NewBird AI, a regular session rip that's now colliding with skepticism about how quickly the company can execute.

  • Allbirds stock is among today’s weakest performers. Why is BIRD stock dropping?

Allbirds Bets On AI Compute Pivot With $50 Million Facility

Allbirds Remains Extremely Extended After Explosive Rally

Allbirds is still trading in the upper half of its 52-week range ($2.15 to $24.31), which keeps the longer-term trend tilted upward even after the pre-market pullback. The stock is trading 300% above its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 276.1% above its 100-day SMA, a setup that points to extreme upside extension versus recent trend baselines.

The relative strength index (RSI), a momentum gauge, is 93.78, which is deep in overbought territory and often aligns with "crowded" momentum conditions. RSI at 93.78 shows buyers have been in control to an unusual degree, but it also raises the odds of sharp swings if sentiment cools.

  • Key Resistance: $24.50 — near the 52-week high zone where rallies have recently topped out.
  • Key Support: $5.50 — near the 200-day SMA area that often acts as a longer-term trend line.

Allbirds Pulls Back Sharply In Thursday Pre-Market Trading

BIRD Stock Price Activity: Allbirds shares were down 30.14% at $11.87 at the time of publication on Thursday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Marsh Q1 2026 Earnings Call: Complete Transcript
data.description:
Marsh (NYSE:MRSH) released first-quarter financial results and hosted an earnings call on Thursday. Read the complete transcript below.

This content is powered by Benzinga APIs. For comprehensive financial data and transcripts, visit https://www.benzinga.com/apis/.

Access the full call at https://edge.media-server.com/mmc/p/iwe8ekjc/

Summary

Marsh reported a strong financial performance for Q1 2026, with an 8% increase in revenue to $7.6 billion and an adjusted EPS growth of 8%.

The company is focused on leveraging AI across three main pillars: growth, productivity, and efficiency, with initiatives like AI-enabled applications generating new revenue streams.

Despite soft insurance and reinsurance market conditions, Marsh remains optimistic about future growth, expecting similar revenue growth and margin expansion in 2026 as in 2025.

Strategic leadership changes were announced, aimed at driving growth and enhancing client experience.

The company repurchased $750 million of its stock in Q1 and plans to deploy $5 billion in capital throughout 2026 across dividends, acquisitions, and share repurchases.

Full Transcript

John Doyle (President and CEO)

Mark McGivney (Chief Operating Officer and Chief Financial Officer)

John Doyle (President and CEO)

Thank you, Mark Andrew, we're ready to begin the Q and A session.

OPERATOR

Greg Peters (Equity Analyst)

John Doyle (President and CEO)

OPERATOR

Our next question comes from the line of Mike Zyrimski with bmo.

Thank you, Nick. You're starting to sound like an insurance broker. Dean, any thoughts from Guy Carpenter?

Terrific, Gene. Pat, how are we using AI at Mercer?

Pat Tomlinson (CEO of Mercer)

John Doyle (President and CEO)

it's very targeted to them and very client centric. Thank you Patrick Ted, welcome to the call. Want to share some thoughts of why we're excited about AI at Oliver Wyman?

Ted Moynihan (CEO of Marsh Management Consulting)

John Doyle (President and CEO)

Thank you, Ted. Sorry Mike, for that long answer. I just want to make sure everyone realizes why we're so excited and why we think we're best positioned to deliver greater value than we ever have to our clients and to our shareholders. Do you have a follow up, Mike?

Mike Zyrimski (Equity Analyst)

Yeah, really quick, those helpful follow up just on the pace of Marsh's hiring in terms of the producer level, do you expect that trajectory to change materially in 26 and if yes, higher or lower?

John Doyle (President and CEO)

OPERATOR

Our next question comes from the line of Brian Meredith with ubs.

Brian Meredith (Equity Analyst)

Yes, thanks. A couple of them here for you, John. First one, I'm just curious, given the level of rate decreases that we're seeing out there, what are you seeing with respect to client demand at Marsh given this uncertain kind of macro environment, are you using savings to purchase more coverage? Are they kind of holding back right now to see how the year kind of unfolds? Yeah, I don't know.

John Doyle (President and CEO)

Nick Studer (CEO of Marsh Risk)

Dean Klissura (CEO of Guy Carpenter)

John Doyle (President and CEO)

Thanks, Dean.

Brian Meredith (Equity Analyst)

OPERATOR

Our next question comes from the line of Rob Cox with Goldman Sachs.

Rob Cox (Equity Analyst)

Hey, thanks. Good morning. First question I had for you was just going back to the capital deployment and MA side. I'm just curious how, if at all, AI is changing the MA strategy. Are you staying away from certain businesses pivoting towards others and have your technology requirements or anything else changed?

Rob Cox (Equity Analyst)

Our next question comes from the line of Meyer Shields with kbw.

Meyer Shields (Equity Analyst)

Great, thanks so much and good morning. First question for John. I completely get the increasing benefits that you're going to be able to or increasing value that you're going to be able to bring to clients and carriers through AI.

John Doyle (President and CEO)

Meyer Shields (Equity Analyst)

as we call

deals we've been done as a firm over the last several years. And we've also been consciously making organic investments in trying to build out our capabilities broadly around being a main investment player.

OPERATOR

Thanks, Pat. Thanks, Mayor Andrew. Next question, please. Our next question comes from the line of lease Greenspan with Wells Fargo.

Elyse Greenspan

Hi, thanks. Good morning. My first question is on Guy Carpenter. So you guys were at 2% for the quarter and I think you did point out the elevated comp at 5% last Q1. I believe you were at 5% right. Throughout last year. So does a 2% feel like where this business should trend? I guess, at least in the near term, given it sounds like your pricing views are, if anything, pointing to things getting a little bit worse post the 1:1 renewals.

Right. So we've been planning for that and guiding to that. Do you have a follow up, Elyse?

John Doyle (President and CEO)

Yeah. My second question is just on capital. Right. You guys were more active in the Q1 relative to prior first quarters. Obviously we've seen a pullback in the stock price and just the group in general as you guys think about balancing M and A potential as well as where your stock is. Could this be a year, I guess, where you continue to front load? I guess more buybacks, even a little bit more independent of what's going on on the M and A side.

Mark McGivney (Chief Operating Officer and Chief Financial Officer)

David Mozamiden (Equity Analyst)

John Doyle (President and CEO)

OPERATOR

Ladies and gentlemen, this does conclude today's conference. You may now disconnect.

data.title: Full Transcript: ManpowerGroup Q1 2026 Earnings Call
data.description:
On Thursday, ManpowerGroup (NYSE:MAN) discussed first-quarter financial results during its earnings call. The full transcript is provided below.

This transcript is brought to you by Benzinga APIs. For real-time access to our entire catalog, please visit https://www.benzinga.com/apis/ for a consultation.

View the webcast at https://edge.media-server.com/mmc/p/wtomxid8/

Summary

ManpowerGroup reported Q1 revenues of $4.5 billion, reflecting a 3% organic constant currency growth; system-wide revenue, including franchises, was $5 billion.

The company announced a strategic Global Transformation program expected to deliver $200 million in cost savings by 2028, focusing on back and front office processes and leveraging AI.

Improved momentum in key markets including the US, France, and Italy, with strong performance in the manpower segment and stable trends in Experis and Talent Solutions.

Management expressed optimism about future growth driven by improving business confidence, with expectations for sustainable organic revenue growth and continued revenue trend improvements in Q2 2026.

Operational highlights include the successful implementation of AI initiatives, such as an AI-powered sales targeting engine in France, contributing to incremental revenue, and global expansion plans for these technologies.

Full Transcript

OPERATOR

Jonas Prising

Jack McGinnis (Chief Financial Officer)

Jonas Prising

Becky Frankiewicz (President and Chief Strategy Officer)

Jack McGinnis (Chief Financial Officer)

OPERATOR

Andrew Steinerman (Equity Analyst at JP Morgan)

Hi. So it's good to be back to growth here and thinking about the guide of organic constant currency, same day basis of 3%. It's pretty similar to the first quarter. Would you call manpower's business in a recovery mode, like leaning towards acceleration here, or would you more call it at a point of stable growth?

Jonas Prising

OPERATOR

Thank you. Our next question comes from Jeff Silber with bmo. Your line is open.

Jonas Prising

Jack McGinnis (Chief Financial Officer)

OPERATOR

Thanks. Thank you. Our next question comes from Kartik Mehta with North Coast Research. Your line is open.

Kartik Mehta (Equity Analyst)

OPERATOR

Thank you. Our next question comes from Mark Marcon with Baird. Your line is open.

Jonas Prising

Jack McGinnis (Chief Financial Officer)

OPERATOR

Thank you. Our next question comes from George Tong with Goldman Sachs. Your line is open.

Jack McGinnis (Chief Financial Officer)

George Tong (Equity Analyst)

Got it. Very helpful, thank you.

OPERATOR

Thank you. Our next question comes from Manav Patnik with Barclays. Your line is open.

Ronan Kennedy (Equity Analyst)

Hi, good morning, this is Ronan Kennedy, Alpha Manav, thank you for taking my questions. If not mistaken, you referenced $200 million of incremental revenue in France from AI powered sales. How scalable is this globally and which markets represent the next largest opportunity and then beyond that top line contribution, how is AI changing win rates, pricing discipline, customer lifetime values and when can we expect to see this reflected in margins?

Becky Frankiewicz (President and Chief Strategy Officer)

Ronan Kennedy (Equity Analyst)

Thank you, Becky. I appreciate it.

OPERATOR

Thank you. Our next question comes from Toby Sommer with Truist. Your line is open. Thanks.

Jonas Prising

I wanted to ask you a relative question on your AI targeting tool as well as your systems investments and reimagining. Where do you think this puts you in terms of market share and let's say the lead on reimagining versus others? After three or four years of declining market, there are probably a lot of boards and management teams in front of a whiteboard trying to reimagine. And where do you think you are relative to their visions of the future and actions?

Becky Frankiewicz (President and Chief Strategy Officer)

Toby Sommer (Equity Analyst)

Okay, thank you.

OPERATOR

Thank you. Our next question comes from Trevor Romeo with William Blair. Your line is open. Thanks for taking the questions. Just one quick one for me. I was wondering if you could talk about. Hey, Trevor, sorry, we could not hear any, any of that questions. Could you please repeat the question? You were breaking up.

Trevor Romeo (Equity Analyst)

Yeah, hopefully this is better. Thank you for taking the question. I was trying to ask about the overall environment for experis in the U.S. sounds like you're expecting things to get up a little bit.

Becky Frankiewicz (President and Chief Strategy Officer)

Jack McGinnis (Chief Financial Officer)

OPERATOR

Thank you. Our next question comes from Josh Chan with ubs. Your line is open.

Josh Chan (Equity Analyst)

Hi, good morning Jonas, Becky, Jack, thanks for taking my question on the savings. Could you just give more color in terms of what is actually being saved to result in the Dollar savings. And then relatedly, kind of conceptually, why would the savings be higher in the front office than the back office? Thank you.

Jack McGinnis (Chief Financial Officer)

Josh Chan (Equity Analyst)

Great. Thank you for the call, Jack.

OPERATOR

Thank you. I'm showing no further questions at this time. I'd like to turn the call back over to Jonas Prezing for closing remarks.

Jonas Prising

Thanks, Michelle. And thanks everyone for participating in our earnings call this morning. We look forward to speaking with you again at our Q2 earnings call in July. And until then, thanks very much. Look forward to speaking with all of you again soon.

OPERATOR

Thank you for your participation. You may now disconnect. Everyone. Have a great day.
